plainMeaning: Nectar dwells in Gautama's thumb and he holds a treasury of spiritual powers, so let us remember this teacher Gautama, who grants whatever good thing we wish for.
recitation: aṅgūṭhe amṛta vase, labdhi-taṇā bhaṇḍāra; te guru gautama samarīe, vāñchita phala dātāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Locative of Gujarati aṅgūṭho, from Sanskrit aṅguṣṭha (thumb, great toe); the loose locative -e marks the seat where the nectar resides.
wordByWord.notes: Points to the famous tale of Gautamasvāmī's labdhi by which a single touch of his thumb to food multiplied it to feed 1500 ascetics (the akṣīṇa-mahānasī labdhi); the thumb is thus the bodily emblem of his attainments.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit a- (not) + mṛta (dead): the deathless draught; here the life-giving grace concentrated in the guru.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; here a concrete metaphor, not the literal soma.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati vasvuṁ, from Sanskrit root vas (to dwell, abide); 3rd-person present.
wordByWord.notes: The nectar is said to abide permanently, not merely visit — a steady wellspring.

wordByWord.etymology: labdhi (Sanskrit, from labh, 'to obtain' = supernormal attainment) + taṇā, the Old/classical Gujarati genitive postposition 'of', descended from the Apabhramsa genitive -āṇa.
wordByWord.notes: taṇā agrees with the following bhaṇḍāra; 'a treasury whose contents are labdhis.' labdhi is a preface glossary term.

wordByWord.etymology: From Sanskrit bhāṇḍāgāra (bhāṇḍa, 'goods/vessel' + āgāra, 'house') = storehouse, treasury.
wordByWord.notes: Gautama is not merely endowed with a labdhi but is their whole repository — an image of inexhaustible spiritual wealth.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati demonstrative te, from Sanskrit tad; here pointing back to the figure just described.
wordByWord.notes: Functions as 'that (very) Gautama' — the deictic gathers the two epithets of line 1 onto the named guru.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit guru ('weighty, venerable'), the spiritual guide.
wordByWord.notes: Frames Gautama not only as gaṇadhara but as the reciter's own guru-figure to be invoked.

wordByWord.etymology: Gautama, gotra-name of Indrabhūti, Mahāvīra's chief gaṇadhara.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; the central object of devotion in this dohā.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati samarvuṁ, from Sanskrit smṛ / smaraṇa (to remember, call to mind); hortative-optative 'let us remember'.
wordByWord.notes: The grammatical heart of the verse: the action it commends is smaraṇa (preface glossary) — devotional remembrance, the engine of the boon.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit past participle of vāñch (to wish, desire); 'that which is longed for'.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies phala; the desire is left open — spiritual or worldly — which line 4 then unpacks into concrete boons.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit phala (fruit, reward, result of an action).
wordByWord.notes: The 'fruit' of the act of remembrance — outcome-language familiar from karma-phala.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit dātṛ (agent noun from dā, 'to give'), nominative dātā, here in the vernacular form dātāra.
wordByWord.notes: In apposition to Gautama: he is himself the bestower of the wished-for fruit — devotion's results are credited to his grace.

literalTranslation: In (his) thumb dwells nectar; (he is) a treasury of attainments. Let us remember that teacher Gautama, (the) giver of the wished-for fruit.
idiomaticTranslation: Nectar abides in his very thumb; he is an overflowing treasury of spiritual powers. Let us call to mind that teacher Gautama — the bestower of every fruit our heart desires.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the previous verse simply enumerated great souls to be remembered at dawn, this verse halts on one of them and turns enumeration into adoration. Its craft is a tight two-step: line one supplies two epithets — nectar-in-the-thumb and treasury-of-labdhis — and line two collects them with the demonstrative te ('that very one') onto the name Gautama, then states the payoff. The thumb-image is not decorative: in the cherished tradition Gautamasvāmī's thumb, touched to a single bowl, fed fifteen hundred ascetics (the akṣīṇa-mahānasī labdhi), so amṛta vase ('nectar dwells') localizes inexhaustible, life-sustaining grace in the most ordinary bodily point. The second epithet widens the lens from one power to all of them — he is their bhaṇḍāra, the storehouse itself. The grammatical pivot is samarīe, a gentle hortative 'let us remember': the verse does not ask for ritual feats but for smaraṇa, simple loving recollection, and credits the entire result to the guru as dātāra ('giver'). Note the deliberate openness of vāñchita phala — 'the wished-for fruit', whatever it may be — which keeps this verse universal and primes the next, where that open 'fruit' is spelled out as food, a thriving home, and prosperity. So this dohā is the hinge of the central pair: it establishes WHO is to be remembered and WHY he can grant, before the following verse says WHAT is granted.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain doctrine of labdhi — supernormal attainments that arise as by-products of austerity and right conduct, of which Gautamasvāmī is the paradigm. Doctrinally important is the restraint of the claim: the boons are framed as the fruit of smaraṇa directed at a liberated/great soul, i.e. as the auspicious effect of devotion, not as a deity's intervention — Gautama is a gaṇadhara, a perfected human exemplar, not a wish-granting god, and the 'giving' is the natural fruition of the devotee's own oriented mind. The amṛta metaphor likewise stays within Jain bounds: it signifies deathless spiritual sustenance and the soul's own immortal nature, not a literal elixir.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited as the dawn rite closes, this dohā gives the practitioner a single, memorable focus to carry into the day: not a long formula but one act — remember Gautama. It models how a Jain lay reciter may hold a great soul in mind at the threshold of activity, trusting that an auspiciously oriented heart, rather than anxious striving, is what ripens into 'the wished-for fruit.' Many keep it as a stand-alone japa-line outside pratikramaṇa as well.

crossReferences.text: Verse 4 of this sutra (Gāmataṇo pesāraṇo…), which itemizes the 'wished-for fruit' left open here as food, household welfare and prosperity
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: The akṣīṇa-mahānasī labdhi of Gautamasvāmī (feeding 1500 with the touch of his thumb), traditional Gautama-caritra

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots and senses of amṛta, vas, labh/labdhi, bhāṇḍāgāra, vāñch/vāñchita, phala, smṛ, dā/dātṛ.

sources.title: Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Gujarati dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa glosses for labdhi and the vernacular forms (samarīe, taṇā, dātāra) of this dohā.

sources.title: JainQQ digital Jain library (Gautamasvāmī stuti / labdhi accounts)
sources.consultedFor: Verification of the akṣīṇa-mahānasī labdhi tradition behind the 'nectar in the thumb' image and the standing of this dohā as a traditional Gautama stuti.

notes: The verse is a widely circulated anonymous/traditional Gautamasvāmī dohā in classical-Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa register; readings follow the book verbatim (e.g. અમૃત, સમરીએ, વાંછિત). લબ્ધિતણા is written as one orthographic token but parses as labdhi + the genitive postposition taṇā ('of'), agreeing with bhaṇḍāra. The 'nectar in the thumb' alludes to the akṣīṇa-mahānasī labdhi; that hagiographic detail is traditional and not asserted by the book itself, so it is marked traditional. Should the book's footnotes carry a pāṭhāntara for any token, that variant governs.
